Hazard Profile Summary: Compact DU-shielded device. Bare source lethal within minutes. ARS risk.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 30m; call NRC; do NOT approach bare source

Hazard Profile Summary: Retractable rod; hazard if severed/crushed. Check source internal.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – 10m cordon; call NRC; do not retract rod manually

Hazard Profile Summary: Rugged MP35N capsule in logging string. Lost-in-hole scenario = orphan source.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 30m; neutron hazard; NRC/operator notification

Hazard Profile Summary: Double-encapsulated stainless steel. Severe burns if unshielded.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 15m; gamma survey; NRC notification

Hazard Profile Summary: Megadose facility. 192 Co-60 sources focused on single isocenter. ARS or fatal if accessed.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE facility; do NOT enter treatment room; call RSO + NRC

Hazard Profile Summary: Guide wire seed. Amputations/fatal ARS if bare wire handled directly.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – Do NOT handle wire; call RSO; use long-handle tool

Hazard Profile Summary: Massive shielded unit. External handling safe. Breach by heavy machinery = fatal exposure.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE 50m; DO NOT open; RSO + NRC immediately

Hazard Profile Summary: Pure beta emitters. Skin/eye hazard if beam window breached. No whole-body depth hazard.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Beta survey; PPE; limit skin exposure; NRC notification

Hazard Profile Summary: Alpha emitter bonded to foil strips. Zero external hazard. Highly toxic if scraped/ingested.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Do not scrape; alpha wipe test; PPE gloves

Hazard Profile Summary: Gigantic orphan-source risk. Lethal bremsstrahlung. History of fatal scavenger exposures (Georgia 1997).
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 100m; X-ray + beta hazard; NOTIFY IAEA

Hazard Profile Summary: Epoxy-sealed plastic disk. Negligible dose for casual handling.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – No immediate action; log and secure

Hazard Profile Summary: Alpha only; plastic housing prevents exposure. Ingestion/inhalation risk if dismantled.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – No action (intact); collect for disposal; do not dismantle

Hazard Profile Summary: Crawler camera used inside pipelines for weld inspection. Bare source fatal within seconds at close range.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 50m; survey with meter; NRC + operator call

Hazard Profile Summary: Used for thick-section weld radiography. Long half-life = persistent orphan source risk.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 100m; very high penetration; NRC notify

Hazard Profile Summary: Non-contact level measurement on vessels/bunkers. Source in locked housing; beam collimated.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Avoid primary beam; process interlock check; survey

Hazard Profile Summary: High-energy gamma for steel vessel penetration. Used in steel mills/refineries.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – ISOLATE beam path; gamma survey; process shutdown

Hazard Profile Summary: Preferred for 4–30mm steel welds. Smaller exclusion zone than Ir-192. Type A transport container.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 30m; gamma survey; NRC notify

Hazard Profile Summary: Thin-wall (2–10mm) steel inspection. Low energy allows tight collimation. Short half-life = rapid decay.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – ISOLATE 15m; low-energy gamma; lead shield adequate

Hazard Profile Summary: Very thin-wall (2–12mm) steel. Primarily beta emitter. Low dose rate at distance but high on contact.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – ISOLATE 15m; beta survey; lead shield adequate

Hazard Profile Summary: Dual-photon absorptiometry source. Low energy gamma; excellent shielding by lead < 1mm. Replaced by X-ray DEXA.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Low dose; survey; notify RSO

Hazard Profile Summary: Megadose teletherapy. Catastrophic if shielding compromised. Historical accidents: Ciudad Juarez 1962, Goiania 1987 type.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE 100m; co-60 penetrates all common shielding; NRC + RSO

Hazard Profile Summary: Large-activity Cs-137 source for preventing transfusion-associated graft-vs-host disease. Significant orphan source concern.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE; CsCl contamination risk; HAZMAT + NRC

Hazard Profile Summary: Research/lab irradiator. Dense lead/steel shielding. Core access lethal.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE; gamma survey; RSO + NRC

Hazard Profile Summary: Longer-lived alternative to Ir-192 HDR. Used where infrequent source replacement preferred.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – Bare source = EVACUATE; use RSO long-handle tool

Activity (Typical): ~0.4–0.6 mCi per seed (15–22 MBq); 40–120 seeds implanted
Hazard Profile Summary: Titanium-encapsulated seeds. Low external hazard; cremation/autopsy precautions needed.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Handle with forceps; no bare hands; monitor for seed migration

Activity (Typical): 1.4 mCi per seed (52 MBq); multiple seeds implanted
Hazard Profile Summary: Similar to I-125 seeds. Very short range. Precautions for handling/disposal.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Forceps handling; notify RSO; TG-43 protocol

Hazard Profile Summary: Alpha-thermopile pacemaker. Implanted in ~4,000 patients 1970s. Alpha contained by encapsulation. Cremation/autopsy risk.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Do NOT cremate without HEPA; notify NRC; treat as radioactive implant at autopsy

Hazard Profile Summary: Legacy intracavitary/interstitial devices. Many unaccounted. Radon gas daughter product risk.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Gamma survey; ventilation (radon); gloves; call RSO + NRC

Hazard Profile Summary: Cow-and-calf generator. Tc-99m is most common nuclear medicine isotope. Mo-99 column decays rapidly.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Lead pig handling; survey after elution; radiopharmacy SOP

Hazard Profile Summary: Very short HL. PET scanner shielding critical. Positron annihilation = 511 keV gamma pairs. Cyclotron proximity hazard.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Syringe shield mandatory; survey; dose will decay rapidly

Hazard Profile Summary: Patient becomes a radioactive source post-administration. Vomit, saliva, urine all contaminated. Special room required.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Lead apron; patient room contaminated; thyroid blocking for responders

Hazard Profile Summary: Pure low-energy beta. External hazard essentially zero. Internal hazard if tube broken and tritium inhaled/ingested.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Ventilate if broken; no external dose; avoid inhalation

Hazard Profile Summary: Legacy consumer items. Ra-226 dust inhalation hazard if dial fractured. Many in collections. Radon off-gassing.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Do not handle cracked dials; radon survey in storage; gloves + mask

Hazard Profile Summary: Low-energy beta. Skin dose if window damaged. Ingestion hazard. Short half-life moderates long-term concern.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Beta survey; no whole-body hazard; PPE gloves

Hazard Profile Summary: Electron capture detector foil. 100-yr half-life. Contained hazard; no external dose.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – No action if intact; dispose via licensed waste contractor

Hazard Profile Summary: Multi-nuclide calibration disc. Minimal hazard in intact form. Gamma spectrometry reference standard.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Handle by disk edge; no bare hands; log and secure

Hazard Profile Summary: Portable device. Staff theft/loss historically common. Rod retraction system; surface-reading mode.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Neutron and gamma survey; secure; NRC notification

Dose Rate @ 1m (Bare/Unshielded): Core: lethal in fraction of a second
Hazard Profile Summary: Massive pool-type pencil irradiator. Dual-key interlock. Multiple fatalities in history (El Salvador 1989, Decatur IL 1974).
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE all personnel; lethal in seconds; NRC + law enforcement

Hazard Profile Summary: Used to color topaz/diamonds and crosslink polymers. Source pencils in water pool.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE; same as sterilization irradiator; NRC notify

Hazard Profile Summary: Glass microspheres injected into hepatic artery. Bremsstrahlung X-ray hazard from vial. Patient excretes minimal dose.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Acrylic/lead shield for vial; gloves; patient bodily fluids contaminated

Hazard Profile Summary: Resin microspheres; higher embolization than TheraSphere. Lead pot transport.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Lead pot; gloves; patient body waste contaminated

Dose Rate @ 1m (Bare/Unshielded): Calibrated output; safe at arms length
Hazard Profile Summary: Used to calibrate RPMs at borders/ports. Stored in secure containers.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Secure in lead pig; survey; notify RSO

Activity (Typical): ~10.9 kg Pu-238 per RTG (~4,400W thermal initial)
Hazard Profile Summary: General Purpose Heat Source RTG. Alpha emitter; catastrophic if fuel dispersed in launch accident.
Shielding Material: Ir metal cladding + graphite impact shell
First Responder Action Code: 1 – Do NOT approach if damaged; alpha dispersal; FEMA + DOE

Hazard Profile Summary: SNAP-9A failed re-entry (1964) released Pu-238 globally. TRANSIT satellite series.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – Historical; if found: ISOLATE; DOE + NRC notify

Device Name: DU Anti-Armor Penetrator (30mm PGU-20 / A-10 Warthog)
Activity (Typical): Variable per round; ~300 g DU per round
Dose Rate @ 1m (Bare/Unshielded): ~1 µSv/h at 1 m intact; dispersal hazard on impact
Hazard Profile Summary: DU pyrophoric on impact; 30% burns releasing uranium oxide aerosol. Used in Kosovo, Iraq, Gulf War. NATO A-10 Warthog GAU-8/A cannon.
Key Regulatory Framework: IAEA 2003 GOV; NATO AC/326 SG-N(03)5; FAO/WHO
Contamination Vector: Alpha + uranium oxide aerosol on impact
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Site survey; alpha wipe tests; DNBI; no bare skin contact; NIOSH PPE

Device Name: DU Tank Armor Insert (M1 Abrams Chobham)
Manufacturer / Owner: US Army / General Dynamics Land Systems
Hazard Profile Summary: Composite DU-depleted uranium armor sandwiched in Chobham armor array. Alpha emitter externally negligible; hazard only if damaged/burning.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Alpha survey if vehicle burning; DNBI; avoid inhalation

Activity (Typical): 12–83 lbs (5.5–37.7 kg) per weight; 692–1059 lbs total per aircraft
Hazard Profile Summary: Used in 747 built 1968–1981 (21–316 counterweights per tail assembly). Replaced by tungsten post-1981. Crash/fire hazard = uranium oxide dust.
Key Regulatory Framework: NRC ML032180089; FAA AC 20-123; AAIB report
First Responder Action Code: 3 – FAA AC 20-123; alpha wipe; avoid cutting/grinding; NRC notify

Activity (Typical): 11–191 lbs (5–87 kg) per weight; sets >1,000 lbs total
Hazard Profile Summary: DU counterweights used on L-1011, DC-10, 727, JetStar and military aircraft. NRC estimates ~2 million lbs DU in service worldwide (commercial alone).
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Same as 747 DU counterweight protocol; NRC; FAA

Hazard Profile Summary: Luminous dial altimeters, compasses, airspeed indicators. Ra-226 paint still active; radon gas off-gassing. Many in museum/private collections. NRC notified multiple museums.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Do not open; radon survey storage area; gloves; NRC notification

Hazard Profile Summary: Check/calibration source for AN/PDR-27 and AN/PDR-60 radiac sets. Used to verify meter function.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Handle as low-level source; TM procedures

Activity (Typical): Small alpha check source in rotating jig (~exempt quantity)
Hazard Profile Summary: Used with AN/PDR-77 alpha probe calibration. Pu-239 alpha jig mounted in calibration fixture. Alpha hazard if source damaged.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Alpha survey; no bare hands; Army disposal procedures

Hazard Profile Summary: ~4,000+ implanted 1970s–1980s. Alpha fully contained by Ti/Pt housing. Hazard = cremation (HEPA filter req.) or post-mortem handling. Cannot be MRI scanned.
Key Regulatory Framework: NRC 10 CFR 35; Circ. Suppl. 1998; Greatbatch patent
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Do NOT cremate; autopsy precaution; HEPA; NRC Form 748

Hazard Profile Summary: Coin-sized nuclear battery; 100 µW, 3V, 50-year lifespan. Diamond semiconductor converter. Civilian IoT/medical device applications emerging.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – No action; handle as electronic device; emerging tech

Hazard Profile Summary: Research stage betavoltaic using C-14 (nuclear waste byproduct). Ruthenium dye + TiO2 converter. Theoretical millennia lifespan.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Research lab SOP; LSC if spill; standard rad lab precautions

Dose Rate @ 1m (Bare/Unshielded): Negligible external; dust inhalation risk during sharpening
Hazard Profile Summary: Standard TIG welding electrode. ThO2 lowers work function. Major hazard = grinding dust inhalation (alpha carcinogen). >1 billion electrodes in use globally.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – N95/P100 mask when grinding; local exhaust ventilation; dispose via licensed contractor

Dose Rate @ 1m (Bare/Unshielded): Negligible external; inhalation hazard if burned mantle ash disturbed
Hazard Profile Summary: Thoriated mantles still sold in some markets. Combustion concentrates Th daughters in ash. Korean regulation: max 700 Bq/g Th.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Do not burn in enclosed space; do not inhale ash; dispose via licensed contractor

Activity (Typical): 7.4 GBq (200 mCi) per dose cycle; 4 cycles
Hazard Profile Summary: Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy for NETs. Patient bodily fluids contaminated 24–72h post-dose. Lead/acrylic shielding during dispensing.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Lead+acrylic shield; patient urine/feces contaminated 48h; thyroid not at risk; survey room post-patient

Activity (Typical): 7.4 GBq (200 mCi) per dose; up to 6 cycles
Hazard Profile Summary: FDA-approved 2022 for mCRPC. Similar handling to Lutathera. Patient isolation for 24h post-infusion. Dosimetry imaging required.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Same as Lu-177 DOTATATE; patient isolation 24h; NRC authorized user required

Activity (Typical): 6 mL vial, 1,000 kBq/mL (27 µCi/mL) at reference date; 50 kBq/kg dose
Hazard Profile Summary: Alpha emitter mimics calcium; targets bone mets. Excreted via GI tract. Stool contamination up to 2 weeks post-dose. Lead shielding for vial.
Key Regulatory Framework: FDA PMA 2013; NRC 10 CFR 35.1000; Bayer PI 2013
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Lead vial shielding; patient stool contaminated; alpha wipe of room; gloves

Activity (Typical): Calibrated per applicator; dose rate ≥6,000 cGy/h (posterior); ≥25,000 cGy/h (anterior)
Hazard Profile Summary: Concave applicator pressed to eye surface. Curved shapes (SIA, SIB, etc.). Pure beta = rapid drop-off in tissue.
Shielding Material: Applicator handle (no shielding needed far from surface)
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Forceps; do not contact working face; beta survey; return to shielded case

Activity (Typical): Calibrated per plaque; apex dose ~100–141 Gy; scleral dose ~558 Gy
Hazard Profile Summary: Gold-backed Ru-106 concave plaque. Sutured to sclera for days. Standard in Europe/Asia for uveal melanoma ≤5 mm depth.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Forceps; gold plaque provides some shielding; beta survey; OR precautions

Hazard Profile Summary: Novel high-dose-rate single-session treatment for choroidal melanoma. Very short treatment time (3–4 min). Pure beta.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Very short procedure; acrylic shield; beta survey; rapid decay

Activity (Typical): ~2 GBq per treatment dose; resin microspheres
Hazard Profile Summary: Higher embolization effect vs TheraSphere. Used for mCRC liver mets. NRC authorized user required.
First Responder Action Code: 3 – Lead pot; gloves; patient body waste contaminated

Dose Rate @ 1m (Bare/Unshielded): ~50,000 µSv/h neutrons at 1 m (2 mCi src)
Hazard Profile Summary: Cf-252 emits 1012 n/s per gram via spontaneous fission. Shorter HL = lower orphan source liability vs Am-Be. Used in UAE ADCO operations.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 30m; neutron hazard; NRC + operator; do NOT approach without TEPC

Hazard Profile Summary: Prompt gamma neutron activation analysis for on-belt analysis of coal, cement, minerals. Fixed installation.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Fixed installation; do not enter during operation; neutron survey; lock-out tag-out

Hazard Profile Summary: Widely used in US research institutions. Multiple units cited in IAEA orphan source concerns. Cesium chloride form = highly dispersible.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE; CsCl = dispersible RDD material; NRC + law enforcement + HAZMAT

Hazard Profile Summary: CsCl form in stainless capsules. Significant security concern due to cesium chloride dispersibility. NRC ordered improved security 2012.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – EVACUATE; same as Shepherd; NRC + law enforcement

Activity (Typical): Up to 20 Ci (740 GBq); 2.2×10^6 n/s per Ci
Hazard Profile Summary: 432-year half-life = persistent orphan source. Lost-in-hole scenario catastrophic. Dual-encapsulated MP35N capsule.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 30m; neutron survey; NRC notify; 432yr orphan source

Hazard Profile Summary: Legacy Pu-239/Be sources in well logging and calibration. Extremely long half-life. Classified as Category 2 by IAEA.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – ISOLATE 30m; neutron + alpha hazard; classified source; DOE + NRC

Hazard Profile Summary: Industrial version of Am-241 ionizing bar. Alpha only; zero external hazard. Toxic if ingested (Litvinenko case demonstrated weaponized form).
Contamination Vector: Alpha (zero external; ingestion lethal ~1µg lethal dose)
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Do not scrape; alpha wipe; HAZMAT if dispersed; Litvinenko precedent

Hazard Profile Summary: Legacy watch industry sources still found in collections. Paint deteriorates; Ra-226 dust. Radon gas daughter off-gassing from storage containers. NRC cleanup programs ongoing.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – Radon survey; gloves; do not handle cracked paint; NRC cleanup program

Hazard Profile Summary: Curium-244 emits neutrons via alpha + fission. Alternative to Cf-252. Shorter half-life than Pu-239/Be.
First Responder Action Code: 2 – ISOLATE 30m; neutron + alpha; DOE authorized use only

Device Name: Selective Internal Radiation Therapy (SIRT) Planning Dose (Tc-99m MAA)
Hazard Profile Summary: Tc-99m labelled macro-aggregated albumin injected before Y-90 to map shunt fraction and predict lung dose.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Syringe shield; survey; low dose; 6-hour decay

Hazard Profile Summary: Nuclear medicine scan for lymphoma, infection. Multi-energy gamma; medium half-life.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Lead container; survey patient room; patient excreta contaminated briefly

Hazard Profile Summary: Nuclear medicine imaging; partly replaced by Ga-68 DOTATATE PET. Medium half-life; patient discharge restrictions.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Lead handling; survey; medium HL means longer patient monitoring

Hazard Profile Summary: Nuclear cardiology. Partly replaced by Tc-99m agents. Cyclotron-produced.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Lead shield; survey patient; excreta monitoring 24h

Hazard Profile Summary: DEXA predecessor. Used pre-1990s. Dual-photon technique; Gd-153 decay produces two usable photon energies.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – Very low dose; survey; <1mm Pb stops beam; NRC 10 CFR 35

Hazard Profile Summary: Most common HDR source globally. Source wire 0.9mm × 3.5mm. Remote afterloading. Retraction failure = patient overexposure.
First Responder Action Code: 1 – Retraction failure = patient left with source; EVACUATE room; RSO; long-handle retrieval

Device Name: Co-57 Flood Source (Nuclear Medicine Camera QC)
Hazard Profile Summary: Used to flood gamma camera detectors for uniformity testing. Low-energy gamma; thin lead shielding sufficient.
First Responder Action Code: 5 – Handle with lead sleeve; gamma survey; NRC 10 CFR 35

Hazard Profile Summary: Used to calibrate/check PET scanners. Long-lived Ge-68 parent. Annihilation pairs require PET-grade shielding.
First Responder Action Code: 4 – PET-grade shielding; survey; Ge-68 parent longevity means persistent source

Hazard (damaged/fire): HIGH — pyrophoric: 20-30% burns on impact; U-oxide aerosol
Contamination Scenario: Impact site: U-oxide dust plume; soil contamination radius ~50m
EOD/CBRN Protocol: Alpha wipe survey; NIOSH DU SOP; do NOT disturb impact craters; PPE Level C minimum
Historical Incidents: Kosovo 1999: UNEP found elevated U levels at 8 of 11 sites; Gulf War: 125 US friendly-fire DU casualties
Country of Use: USA; UK (Challenger 2 CHARM 3 DU rounds); Netherlands (F-16 strafing training)

Contamination Scenario: Vehicle fire: U-oxide fume inside fighting compartment; crew inhalation risk
EOD/CBRN Protocol: Alpha wipe; HEPA vacuum; do NOT enter burning vehicle; FAO-27 protocol
Historical Incidents: Gulf War: >320 US/UK vehicles struck by DU; 100+ contaminated hulks remain in Kuwait

Service / Operator: US Army / General Dynamics Land Systems
Contamination Scenario: Burning vehicle: DU aerosolizes in fire; crew compartment contaminated
EOD/CBRN Protocol: Alpha survey hull exterior; do NOT cut/grind; decontaminate with water
Historical Incidents: T-72 vs M1A1 incidents in Iraq 2003 Gulf War II: contaminated hulks
Notes / Sources: FM 3-3-1; DTRA guidelines; CHPPM Technical Guide 150
Manufacturer / Owner: US Army / General Dynamics Land Systems

Historical Incidents: UK MoD 2001 review; DU health monitoring programme for Gulf War veterans

DU / Activity: 12–83 lbs/weight; 316 max per 747 tail = up to 1,059 lbs total
Regulatory Framework: NRC (general licence <15 lb); exempt set; FAA AC 20-123
Contamination Scenario: Crash with fire: DU oxidizes; U-oxide contamination at crash site radius ~50m
EOD/CBRN Protocol: FAA AC 20-123; DOT ERG; alpha wipe survey at crash site
Historical Incidents: Amsterdam 1992: El Al 747 crash; DU counterweights found in debris; Dutch govt inquiry
Notes / Sources: NRC ML032180089; FAA AC 20-123; Dutch Health Council 1998

DU / Activity: 11–191 lbs per weight; up to 1,000+ lbs per aircraft
Historical Incidents: NRC estimates ~2 million lbs DU in commercial aviation service (1981)

Service / Operator: US Navy/Army Air Forces; RAF; Luftwaffe
Form: Ra-226 luminous paint on dial face
Contamination Scenario: Paint fragmentation; dust inhalation; radon off-gassing in storage containers
EOD/CBRN Protocol: Ventilate storage; alpha survey; gloves; NRC notification if found in quantity
Historical Incidents: Multiple WWII aircraft restoration finds in UK, Australia, USA; NRC museum notifications
Country of Use: USA, UK, Canada, Germany, USSR, Japan
Notes / Sources: NRC ML18131A356; AAIB Special Bulletin S3/2014
Manufacturer / Owner: US Navy/Army Air Forces; RAF; Luftwaffe

Form: Alpha check source disc in calibration jig
EOD/CBRN Protocol: TM procedures; replace expired source through Army supply chain
Historical Incidents: Operational equipment; no incidents on record

Contamination Scenario: 1964 failure: global Pu-238 stratospheric dispersal; still detectable in ice cores
EOD/CBRN Protocol: Historical; DOE archival; no current operational units
Historical Incidents: TRANSIT 5BN-3 re-entry 1964: released ~1 kg Pu-238 globally; measurable global contamination increase

Form: PuO2 ceramic in Ir-clad GPHS module
DU / Activity: ~10.9 kg Pu-238 per RTG × 3 units = ~32.7 kg Pu-238 total (Cassini)
Contamination Scenario: EIS modeled 5 scenarios; worst case: Earth flyby re-entry ~4,800 cancer fatalities expected over 50yr
EOD/CBRN Protocol: FEMA; DOE Emergency Response; no field protocol (classified launch range)
Historical Incidents: Cassini 1997 launch: no accident; flyby 1999 nominal
Notes / Sources: NASA EIS for Cassini 1997; Kathy Schneider NRC; INSRP 1997

Contamination Scenario: Launch abort scenario: localized PuO2 dispersal at launch site; Pu-238 non-fissile
EOD/CBRN Protocol: Launch range procedures; FEMA; no first-responder encounter expected on Earth

Contamination Scenario: Cremation without HEPA: Pu-238 in flue gases; autopsy scalpel penetration theoretically possible (unlikely)
EOD/CBRN Protocol: Notify NRC before cremation; HEPA filter crematorium; NRC Form 748
Historical Incidents: 2003 US crematorium incident: Pu-238 filter bypass detected by air monitor
